actions.thought: In a Heads-Up match with 100 BB stacks, the Small Blind has a wide opening range, typically playing about 80-90% of hands. However, 9-7 offsuit is near the bottom of this range. While it has some connectivity, it lacks the playability of suited variants or higher cards. Without any information on the opponent's tendencies (Hand 1), adopting a slightly conservative baseline is prudent. 97o is marginally -EV to neutral to play, and folding avoids a tricky post-flop spot out of position if the opponent raises or calls. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding 42o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although this is a heads-up situation where ranges are wider, playing such a low-equity hand out of position (pre-flop) or even in position (post-flop) is generally negative expected value. Given it is very early in the session (hand 2 of 150), I prefer to wait for better spots rather than bloating the pot with trash.

In a Heads-Up match, the Button/SB requires a wide opening range, but J3o sits at the very bottom of playable hands (bottom 20%). With 100BB stacks, opening this hand creates negative expected value due to poor post-flop playability and reverse implied odds. Since we are early in the match with no specific reads on the opponent to justify an exploit (like them over-folding), adhering to a solid pre-flop range and folding this trash hand is the disciplined play.

K3o is a standard opening hand from the button in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. While not particularly strong, its high-card value (the King) makes it worth a raise to 3x the big blind to seize initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Opponent raises to 3bb (300) from the Button/SB. I hold Qc2h in the Big Blind. While heads-up requires defending a wide range, Q2o is at the very bottom of playable hands. The 3x sizing worsens my pot odds (requiring 33% equity), and being out of position with a hand that has poor playability and significant reverse implied odds (due to the bad kicker) makes realizing my raw equity difficult. Against a 3bb open, this is a standard fold.

I hold Pocket Nines (9c 9s) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With deep stacks (approx. 100BB), my goal is to build the pot for value and deny free equity to the Big Blind. Limping is passive and risks letting random hands hit the flop cheaply. A raise is the standard play to take initiative and extract value from weaker pairs, Ax, and broadway hands. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to charge the opponent while keeping the pot manageable relative to my hand strength.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand heads-up. The opponent (Pro) has opened for 2.5x from the Small Blind/Button. While calling is an option, I prefer to 3-bet here to take the initiative, define their range, and build a pot with a hand that is likely ahead. I will raise to 850 total to exert pressure.

I hold Pocket Nines (99) in position. After my initial raise to 250, the opponent 3-bet to 850. In heads-up play, 99 is a premium hand, well above the folding threshold. However, 4-betting here often isolates me against a stronger range (TT+, JJ+, QQ+, KK+, AA, AK) where I am either flipping or dominated. Calling is the most balanced option: it keeps the opponent's bluffs in the hand, utilizes my position post-flop, and gives me a Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of roughly 5, which allows for maneuverability. I need to call 600 more into a pot of 1100, offering good odds given the strength of my hand and stack depth.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a board of 10-7-5. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage and the initiative. This board is somewhat connected with heart draws and straight possibilities (89, 68), so I need to bet for value and protection. A bet of 850 (half pot) is a standard continuation bet that charges draws and gets value from weaker Tens or middle pairs.

In a heads-up 3-bet pot, facing a half-pot c-bet on a Tc 7h 5h board, my Pocket Nines are a marginal made hand but too strong to fold immediately. The opponent's range contains many overcards (AK, AQ, KQ) and flush draws that I am currently ahead of. Additionally, I have a gutshot straight draw (an 8 makes a 5-7-8-9-T straight) and 2 outs to a set, giving me roughly 6 outs to improve significantly. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (Tx, JJ+), while folding is too exploitable given the width of heads-up 3-bet ranges. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn and keeps the opponent's bluffs in the pot.

The King of Spades on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Tens and hits a portion of the opponent's calling range from the flop (such as KQ, KJ, or AK). Since I am out of position and the board has become more coordinated with straight and flush draws, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the size of the pot, keep the opponent's bluffs in their range, and evaluate their reaction to the King. Betting here might only get called by better hands while forcing out the draws I currently beat.

On the turn, the King of Spades arrives, making the board Tc 7h 5h Ks. This card hits the opponent's 3-betting range (AK, KQ, KJs) significantly harder than mine. Since the opponent checked, they may be pot-controlling a hand like Tx/JJ, giving up on a pure bluff, or trapping with a King. My hand (99) is now third pair and has mostly showdown value against missed broadway draws or smaller pairs. Betting achieves little: worse hands fold, and better hands call. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to see a river card to potentially improve or get to showdown cheaply against Ace-high type hands.

The river is the Jack of Hearts, which completes the heart flush and potential straights (Q9). My pair of Tens is now third pair on a very wet board, although I do hold the Ace of Hearts blocker. Given that the turn went check-check, it is unlikely the opponent has a King, but they could easily have a Jack, a slow-played set, or have completed a draw on the river. There is no value in betting here, as I'm unlikely to be called by worse. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown or evaluate a bet from the opponent.

I hold Pocket Nines on a very wet board of Tc 7h 5h Ks Jh. The river brings a third heart (completing flushes) and a third broadway card (completing straights for AQ, Q9). My hand is now effectively 4th pair. The opponent 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, giving them a range heavy in broadway cards (AK, AQ, KQ, JJ, TT) which have likely connected with this board as pairs, straights, or better. While the opponent checked the turn and river, showing weakness or pot control, there are almost no hands in a 3-bet range that I beat (except perhaps low suited connecters that missed or non-heart low Ax). Betting accomplishes nothing: worse hands fold, and better hands (which are plentiful) call. My best option is to check back to get to showdown cheaply, hoping to catch a pure bluff, while accepting that I am likely beaten.

In heads-up play, J9o is a strong starting hand from the button. I have a stack advantage and have been playing relatively aggressively. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot, as this hand performs well against a wide big blind defending range.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5s, a suited one-gapper, facing a 3x raise from the Button. In heads-up play, ranges are wide, and 75s has reasonable playability and equity. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400, offering me 2:1 odds (33% equity required). My hand generally realizes equity well enough to justify a call. I will defend my blind and see a flop.

I hold 7s5s on a flop of Ad 4d Qs. I have missed the flop completely, holding only 7-high with no immediate draws (only backdoor straight and flush possibilities). The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who likely has Aces, Queens, or broadway cards in their range. Being out of position with a very weak hand, leading out is not a viable strategy. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If facing a bet, I will likely have to fold, as I have insufficient equity to continue.

As the pre-flop raiser, I have a significant range advantage on an Ace-Queen high board, as my range contains more strong Aces and Queens than the Big Blind's defending range. Although J9o has no current value, a standard continuation bet of 1/3rd the pot (200 chips) is an effective way to capitalize on this advantage and force a fold from my opponent's air and weaker hands. If called, I can evaluate the turn, but the flop is a prime candidate for a C-bet.

I hold 7s5s on an Ad-4d-Qs board. I have missed completely, holding only 7-high with no pair and no immediate draw (only remote backdoor possibilities). The opponent (Flash) has c-bet 200 into 600 (1/3 pot). While the price is cheap, this board texture (Ace-Queen high) interacts very well with the pre-flop raiser's range. Out of position with no equity and no showdown value, continuing is not profitable. The correct play is to fold.

In Heads-Up play, the Button is the Small Blind. I am in position post-flop. 98 suited is a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. Standard strategy dictates opening a wide range from the button, and 98s is near the top of my speculative range. Raising applies pressure and builds a pot where I have the advantage of position. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to deny free equity to the Big Blind and take initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 2s, which is a very weak hand. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 400), 52o is difficult to play out of position and has very little equity. It's better to fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot than to bleed chips post-flop.

I am on the button with J5o. In heads-up play, opening from the button with a wide range is standard to put pressure on the big blind. While J5o is a weak hand, it is playable in position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

Opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. I hold 8d 7h (87 offsuit) in the Big Blind. In Heads-Up play, the button opens a wide range, but the 3bb sizing offers worse pot odds (calling 200 to win 600, requiring 33% equity) compared to a min-raise. While 87o has raw equity of roughly 40% against a wide range, it suffers from poor playability out of position without the flush potential of the suited variant. Realizing equity with offsuit connectors OOP is difficult, and standard defense charts against a 3bb open typically suggest folding hands weaker than 98o. Therefore, folding is the disciplined play to preserve the stack.

I hold 7d 3s on the Button/Small Blind, which is a statistically very weak hand (bottom 10-15% of range). While I have position post-flop in heads-up play, this hand has poor playability due to being unsuited and disconnected. My opponent, Flash, has shown aggression previously (3-betting from the BB in Hand #5), making a limp or raise risky as I would likely have to fold to a re-raise or play a large pot with a trash hand. The standard and most +EV play here is to simply fold and surrender the small blind.

I am on the button with T7o. Given that Pro has folded to several of my button raises in recent hands (Hands 4, 6, and 8), I will maintain my aggressive posture and open-raise. T7o is a reasonable hand to steal the blinds with or play in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to exert pressure and build the pot.

In heads-up play, the button's opening range is typically very wide (often 70%+). J9o is a standard defense hand in the Big Blind. While the opponent is using a larger sizing of 3bb (300 chips) rather than a min-raise, I am getting 2:1 on a call (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring ~33% equity. J9o has approximately 45-48% equity against a standard wide heads-up opening range. Folding here would be over-folding my Big Blind. A call is the most prudent action to see a flop.

I am out of position on a dry T-6-4 rainbow flop with Jack-high (J9o). This board texture does not favor my range significantly over the pre-flop raiser's range. Donk betting here would likely only get called by better hands (Tx, pairs) and fold out worse hands that I am beating or flipping against. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue or take a free card. I will check and re-evaluate based on their action.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten (Th) on a relatively dry board of Tc-6h-4s. My opponent (Pro) checked to me. While my kicker (7) is not very strong, top pair is currently the best hand in most scenarios here. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand from overcards. A bet of around half the pot (300) should get called by middle pairs, draws, or even Ace-high hands, while allowing me to control the size of the pot.

On a T-6-4 rainbow flop, I hold J9o. I have one overcard to the board (Jack) and one overcard to the second pair (Nine), but no immediate straight or flush draws. The opponent bets 300 (half-pot) into a pot of 600. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds, my realizable equity out of position with just overcards and no backdoors (suits don't match for a flush draw) is poor. Floating here would likely lead to losing more chips on future streets unless I hit a Jack or Nine immediately, and even then, I could be dominated. A fold is the standard and prudent play against this c-bet sizing on this texture.

In Heads-Up play, Q8o is well within the top 50% of hands and is a standard opening hand from the Button (Small Blind). The opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression (3-betting Hand #5 and raising 3x consistently from SB), so keeping the raise size small (min-raise) allows me to steal the blind cheaply or play a pot in position, while minimizing the loss if I face a 3-bet and have to fold. I will raise to the minimum allowed.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. This is a standard 2x open. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 300), I only need 25% equity to make this a profitable call. T9o is well above that equity threshold against a typical Button opening range. Additionally, my stack size is healthy, allowing me to see a flop and play post-flop. Calling is the most standard and effective play here to maintain pot control while realizing the equity of my semi-connected cards.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and two overcards on a 3-8-6 board. Standard play from the Big Blind as the defender is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see how the opponent approaches this board texture before deciding whether to continue.

I hold Top Pair (Eights) with a strong Queen kicker on a somewhat wet board (3d 8c 6d) that contains flush and straight draws. In a Heads-Up min-raised pot, this is a strong value hand. Checking back risks giving a free card to overcards (A, K) or allowing draws to complete cheaply. I want to extract value from worse pairs (6x, 3x, weaker 8x) and charge draws. A half-pot bet (200) is a standard size that balances value extraction and protection without over-committing against a check-raise.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and two overcards (Ten and Nine) on a low, somewhat connected board. The opponent has made a half-pot continuation bet of 200 into 400. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, meaning I need 25% equity to continue. While my gutshot alone only gives me about 16% equity on the next card, my overcards provide additional outs, and the board texture is one where the opponent might be betting with a wide range of air or weak draws. Calling allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially realize my equity or bluff on later streets if the board develops favorably.

The turn card is an Eight, pairing the board. I currently hold Ten-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). In the Big Blind, after calling a continuation bet on the flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the board pairing. If they check back, I get a free river card to potentially hit my straight or an overcard. If they bet, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but leading into them here doesn't accomplish much with my current hand strength.

I hold trip Eights with a strong Queen kicker and an 8-high flush draw (8d on 3d 6d board). The turn card (8h) improves my hand to trips, making it very unlikely the opponent has an 8. Consequently, the opponent's range is weighted towards weak pairs (6x, 3x, pocket pairs) or draws. Betting again on the turn likely folds out the weak hands I want to keep in the pot, such as bottom/middle pair. By checking back, I under-represent my hand strength, inducing bluffs on the river from missed draws or extracting value from weak pairs that might call a river bet but fold to a turn barrel. Although giving a free card has risks on this wet board, I have redraws to a flush and full house, and the deception value maximizes expectation against a depleted range.

I have Ten-high with no showdown value on a board of 3-8-6-8-J with three diamonds. I checked the flop and called a continuation bet, then both players checked the turn. The river Jack of diamonds completes a possible flush and is an overcard to the board. Since the opponent checked back the turn, they likely have a marginal made hand with some showdown value, such as a small pair or Ace-high. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the flush or the Jack, my passive line so far makes a bluff less credible. I will check and fold if the opponent bets, as I have no way to win this pot at showdown unless they also have total air and we split or I somehow beat their specific air.

I hold Trip Eights (Qc8d on 3d 8c 6d 8h Jd). While my hand is very strong, the river card (Jd) completes the diamond flush draw. The opponent's range for calling the flop bet heavily consists of flush draws and pairs (6x, 3x, 99-TT). The flush draws have now improved to beat me, and the pairs have likely been scared off by the flush card and the Jack overcard, meaning they will fold to a bet. Betting effectively turns my hand into a bluff catcher or folds out the hands I beat while getting called by flushes. By checking back, I guarantee a win against all non-flush hands (like 6x or missed straight draws) without risking chips against a flush or getting check-raised. The likelihood of getting called by a worse hand (like a hero-calling 6x or non-flush Jx) is low compared to the risk of value-owning myself against a flush.

In heads-up play, a King-high hand on the button is well within the opening range. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard sizing so far, to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand in position.

I hold KdJd in the Big Blind. KJs is a premium hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of a Button opening range. The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb). I have excellent equity to continue. While a 3-bet for value is a viable option to seize initiative, calling is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide, avoid bloating the pot while out of position, and utilize the high playability of suited broadway cards post-flop. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on a relatively dry, rainbow flop of Js-6d-4h. In heads-up play, this is a very strong hand. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Checking allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs and worse value hands (like middle pair or a worse Jack). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs. I intend to check-call if the opponent bets.

I have King-high on a dry board of Js-6d-4h. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Pro checked to me, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) should be effective in folding out many of Pro's hands that missed this board entirely, while allowing me to maintain initiative.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a strong King kicker on a dry, rainbow board (Js-6d-4h). The opponent has continuation bet 300 (half-pot). In this heads-up scenario, my hand is very strong but not invulnerable. Raising (check-raising) here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and isolate me against a range consisting largely of better hands (sets, overpairs) or hands with decent equity. By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or value-betting worse hands on future streets. The board texture is static enough that giving a free card is not a major risk. Therefore, check-calling is the most profitable line.

On the turn, the board is Js-6d-4h-5h. I still hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KdJd). The 5h brings some straight possibilities (78, 37) and a flush draw, but my hand remains very strong in a heads-up pot. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) usually isn't optimal here as it denies the opponent the chance to bluff or value-bet worse hands. If I check, I can comfortably call a bet or value bet the river if it checks through.

I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw. Pro called my continuation bet on the flop, suggesting they have a piece of the board, likely a Jack or a medium pair. The 5h on the turn connects with some of Pro's potential calling range (like 65 or 45). Since Pro checked to me, I will check back to take a free card and realize my equity. Betting again here is risky as Pro's flop-calling range is likely to continue on this turn, and I do not want to get check-raised out of the pot.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a strong King kicker. The river 6c pairs the board, making the final board Js-6d-4h-5h-6c. My hand is effectively Jacks and Sixes with a King kicker. The opponent checked back the turn, which usually caps their range to marginal made hands or draws, indicating they likely do not have a strong hand like a set or overpair. While a thin value bet could target pocket pairs (77-99) or weak Jacks, it would likely fold out all air and missed draws (like hearts). By checking, I give the opponent an opportunity to bluff with their missed draws or air. If they check back, I likely have the best hand at showdown. If they bet, I can comfortably call given their line. Checking maximizes EV by keeping the opponent's range wide.

The river pairs the board with a six. I have King-high with a three, which likely loses to most of Pro's range that called the flop (pairs of Jacks, fours, or fives, and Ace-high). While I could attempt a bluff, Pro's check on the river after calling the flop suggests they might be looking to induce a bet or are prepared to catch a bluff with a marginal hand. Since I checked the turn, my range doesn't look particularly strong either. I will check back to see the showdown and minimize losses, as I still have a healthy chip lead.

I am holding 2d 5s in the Small Blind (Button). This is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Heads-Up Texas Hold'em. While a wide opening range is standard in HU play, 5-2 offsuit is at the very bottom of that range and is a standard fold in equilibrium strategies. My opponent has shown aggression, and playing a weak hand against a larger stack is unnecessary risk. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

94o is one of the weakest starting hands in heads-up play. While I have been successfully stealing blinds from the button in previous hands, I need to maintain some level of range integrity. I previously folded 42o in a similar position. Folding the bottom 20% of my range ensures that my raises continue to exert pressure and prevents me from getting into difficult spots post-flop with zero equity.

I am on the Button (SB) heads-up with King-Deuce suited (Kd2d). This is a playable hand in heads-up poker, falling well within the standard opening range (top 50-60% of hands). My goal is to steal the blind or play a pot in position with the initiative. A minimum raise to 200 is the standard sizing to accomplish this risk-reward balance. If the opponent 3-bets, this hand is easy to fold, but if called, I have flush potential and a King blocker.

I am in the Big Blind with Td9c, which is a solid hand to defend against a minimum raise (2x) from the Small Blind. The pot odds are 3:1 (I need to call 100 to win a total pot of 400), which makes this a very standard call with a hand that has good post-flop playability and connectivity. Folding is too tight, and raising would likely bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding against a competent opponent.

I have flopped top pair on a board that is relatively dry, although it is paired. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. This is a standard play with top pair to allow the opponent to continuation bet with a wider range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out their air and bloat the pot only when I am beaten by a 6 or a better Ten.

On a paired board of 6d-6s-Th, the texture is relatively dry but interacts decently with the Big Blind's calling range (which contains many Tx and 6x hands). As the pre-flop aggressor holding King-high (Kd2d), I have some showdown value beating random Queen/Jack-high floats, but I am unlikely to get called by worse if I bet. Betting effectively turns my hand into a bluff that folds out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (seeing a free Turn for a King or diamond), keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to potentially bluff-catch or delay-c-bet later. K-high is a standard check-back candidate on this texture heads-up.

I currently hold second pair (Tens) with a nine kicker on a board of 6-6-T-J. The Jack on the turn is an overcard to my pair. Since the flop went check-check, it is unlikely my opponent has a monster, but they could have easily picked up a piece of that Jack or be slow-playing a Ten or a Six. By checking, I am exercising pot control and keeping my range protected. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate the size, but my hand is generally strong enough to see a river in a small pot. Betting here might fold out hands I currently beat while only getting called by better (like a Jack or a slow-played Six).

On the Turn, the board is 6d 6s Th Jc. The opponent checks again. I hold King-high (Kd), which has reasonable showdown value in a pot where both players have shown weakness on the flop. The Jack is a card that hits my range, but betting here turns my hand into a bluff, as worse hands (like Q-high) will fold and better hands (any pair) will likely call. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially win at showdown against missed draws or lower high cards, and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand.

The river 3c is a non-threatening card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold second pair (Tens) with a nine kicker. Given the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely my opponent has a very strong hand like a Six or a Jack, though they could have a better Ten or a small pocket pair. By checking, I am inviting a showdown in a small pot or potentially inducing a bluff from an opponent who has missed their cards entirely. My hand has decent showdown value, and betting might only result in getting called by hands that beat me (like JT or AJ) while folding out hands I already beat.

I hold King-high (Kd) on a board of 6d-6s-Th-Jc-3c. The action has checked through on the flop and turn, and the opponent checks to me on the river. This line from the opponent typically indicates a weak range, consisting of missed straight draws, lower high cards, or perhaps a weak Ace-high or small pocket pair attempting to get to showdown. My King-high has sufficient showdown value to beat the lower portion of the opponent's range (Q-high, 9-high, busted straight draws). Betting would likely only fold out hands I already beat or potentially an Ace-high/small pair, but risking chips here is unnecessary when I have a chance to win the pot for free. Checking back is the standard low-variance play to realize equity.

I am holding 2h 3c, which is one of the weakest starting hands. While I am on the button and have been successfully stealing blinds in previous hands (Hand 4, 8), this specific hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. Given that 'Pro' has shown a willingness to defend their big blind in recent hands (Hand 6, 10, 12), I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than bluffing with the bottom of my range.

Current hand is 8d 3d. In Heads-Up play, this hand is marginal. While suited, 8-high is very weak and easily dominated. The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently (calling min-raises in hands 11 and 15, and 3-betting in hand 5). Raising with this hand into a frequent defender is likely -EV as I will often be playing a larger pot with a significant equity disadvantage. Limping is an option, but folding is the most disciplined play to preserve chips for better spots, especially since I am currently at a chip deficit.

I am on the button with QJs, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value and maintain aggression. I have consistently opened to 3x (300 chips) in previous hands when entering from the button, and I will continue this strategy to build the pot with a hand that has strong post-flop playability and high equity against the Big Blind's defending range.

I hold 5s 4d in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). My opponent has been consistently opening to 300. While 54o has some connectivity, it is an off-suit low hand that plays poorly out of position. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400 (33%), but realizing that equity out of position against a larger raise size is difficult. 54o is generally a fold against a 3x open due to reverse implied odds and difficulty in realizing equity. I decide to fold.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Jd 8d, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, this is a respectable hand that plays well in position. Folding is too weak. Limping gives the Big Blind a free look. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or play in position with a hand that has good playability (flush and straight potential). My opponent has defended against min-raises before, but raising to 200 is a standard efficient play to define the range and build a pot in position. I will raise to 200.

I hold King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. The opponent raised to 200 from the Button. I have a significant stack lead and want to capitalize on my hand strength by building the pot and taking the initiative. A 3-bet to 600 is a standard value raise here, forcing the opponent to either fold their weaker opening range or pay more to see a flop while out of position.

I am holding Jd 8d on the Button (Small Blind) and facing a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind after opening to 200. The pot is currently 800 (200 from me + 600 from opponent). It costs me 400 to call, making the pot odds 400 to win 1200, or 3:1. I need approximately 25% equity to call profitably. J8s is a solid suited connector/one-gapper that plays well in position and retains roughly 35-40% equity against a standard heads-up 3-bet range. Raising with 76o from the button is a standard play to apply pressure on the big blind. It's a speculative hand that plays well in position if called, and it allows me to maintain an aggressive image while having a significant chip lead.

On a board of 6c-7s-Ad-Js-4d, I have a pair of sevens (second pair). Both the flop and turn were checked by both players after Pro's pre-flop raise. I hold Top Pair (Aces) which has transitioned into Two Pair (Aces and Kings) with a Jack kicker as the board paired Kings on the river (Board: As 9s Kh Jh Kc). I hold Trips (Queens) with the best kicker (King) on a board of 2h Qc Th Qs. This is an incredibly strong hand.
